1.notes: Par 5 opener with dramatic downhill tee shot. Arnold Palmer design. Waste area runs down right side.

2.notes: Uphill par 3 with bunkers guarding. Requires precise club selection.

hazards.notes: Expansive natural bunker on left side
Forced carry native area before green

3.notes: #1 handicap hole. Par 5 (Par 4 in NCAA). Uphill dogleg left. Expansive natural bunker on left. Forced carry native area and immense bunker on approach. Lay up on second shot if necessary.

4.notes: Short downhill par 4. Don't get too aggressive with tee shot as fairway narrows near green. Consider driving to comfortable yardage. Green has subtle breaks - study from all sides.

hazards.notes: Native bunker protecting right side of green
Tall trees block approach if driven too far right

5.notes: Dogleg right par 4. Landing area is uphill and obstructed from tee. Drive down left center - further right increases chance of being blocked by trees. Native bunker protects right side of green. Chipping from left gives good up-and-down chance.

green.notes: Large green with false front on front right side

hazards.notes: One of the most terrifying bunkers on front nine

6.notes: Short uphill par 3. Consider elevation change and prevailing headwind - it's always blowing harder than you feel from tee. Over-clubbing never hurts as much as under-clubbing. Leaving approach in bunker is a mistake you will regret.

7.notes: Reachable par 5 for long hitters. Water guards aggressive line.

8.notes: Uphill par 3 with elevated green. Club up for elevation.

9.notes: Strong finishing hole for front nine. Water threatens right side.

green.notes: Very large green with subtle breaks. Backstop in back right portion.

hazards.notes: Magnificent but ominous bunker with fingers and islands of native grass

10.notes: Short par 4. Fairway grades sharply to the right - tee shot down left side. Drive to comfortable approach distance. Climbing approach protected by magnificent bunker. Avoid the sand - you don't want ball in bunker or on native grass fingers. Distance control essential on approach to large subtle green.

green.notes: Small green - short, long, left and right all defended by bunkers and native grasses

hazards.notes: Several bunkers safeguarding rising approach from 150 yards in

11.notes: SIGNATURE HOLE. Longest par 5 (665 yards from competition tees). Don't get sidetracked by spectacular view of downtown Raleigh. Second shot placement is key. Small green defended on all sides by bunkers and native grasses. Course design concept shines - cut out of hillside from high point to high point. Views include downtown, Centennial Campus, Bell Tower, and Smokestack.

green.notes: Deep but not very wide - accuracy on approach is a must

hazards.notes: Large native bunker with harsh slope on left side

12.notes: Downhill tee shot with dramatic view of downtown Raleigh. Ball fades through skyline toward native bunker on left. Fairway narrows toward green. Layup to perfect yardage is smart option. No bailout left or right of green.

hazards.notes: Most treacherous native fairway bunker on entire course - steep and jagged

13.notes: Uphill dogleg right par 4. Tee shot is vital - keep left at all costs. The native fairway bunker on right is the most treacherous on entire course - steep and jagged. If you find it, take your medicine and play back to fairway. Fairway tilts considerably near green. Do not hit long - uphill recovery from deep native grass behind green is difficult.

green.notes: Most dramatic green on course with many twists and turns. Ball tends to get away from you.

hazards.notes: Left side slopes severely into hazard

14.notes: Par 3 with most dramatic green on course. Distance and accuracy both critical. Bunker right is daunting but left side slopes severely into hazard. Short of green is not a bad miss. Once on green, settle yourself by looking back at stunning view of hillsides of #16 and #18. Take deep breath and settle for 2-putt - ball will get away if not conscious of twists and turns.

15.notes: Risk-reward par 5. Water right for those cutting corner.

16.notes: Straightaway par 4. Avoid fairway bunker right for clean approach.

17.notes: Shortest hole on course. Water front and left. Precision over power.

18.notes: Dramatic uphill finishing hole. Water left, bunker right. Strong finish.

1.notes: Tea Olive. Wide opening hole. Favor left side off tee to avoid fairway bunker right. Approach uphill to tiered green.

2.notes: Pink Dogwood. Reachable par 5 for long hitters. Uphill approach to large, severely sloped green. Deep bunkers guard front-left and back-right.

3.notes: Flowering Peach. Short par 4 with four bunkers surrounding narrow green. Pin position critical. Most three-putts occur here.

4.notes: Flowering Crab Apple. Longest par 3. Largest green on the course. Slopes severely right to left. Club selection paramount.

5.notes: Magnolia. Demanding dogleg left. Two fairway bunkers guard inside of dogleg. One of the toughest second shots on the course - long and uphill.

6.notes: Juniper. Severe 30-foot drop from tee to green. Take less club than you think. Green slopes dramatically back to front.

7.notes: Pampas. Precision more important than power. Miss the green and you'll face a difficult up-and-down from deep bunkers.

8.notes: Yellow Jasmine. Uphill par 5 through corridor of towering pines. Bunkers guard both sides of landing area. Mound behind green rejects long approaches.

9.notes: Carolina Cherry. Dramatic downhill dogleg. Severely sloped green - back to front and right to left. Strong finish to front nine.

10.notes: Camellia. 700 feet of elevation change on back nine begins here. Dramatically downhill dogleg left. Larry Mize won 1987 Masters from greenside.

11.notes: White Dogwood. AMEN CORNER BEGINS. Pond guards entire left side approaching green. Ben Hogan's famous 1-iron. Larry Mize chip-in 1987.

12.notes: Golden Bell. Most famous par 3 in golf. Rae's Creek crosses in front. Swirling winds make club selection treacherous. Shallowest green on course.

13.notes: Azalea. Amen Corner finale. Sharp dogleg left with Rae's Creek along left and crossing in front of green. Risk-reward par 5. Eagles and big numbers both common.

14.notes: Chinese Fir. ONLY HOLE WITHOUT A BUNKER. Don't be fooled - the green is the defense. Extremely fast, severely contoured. Many three-putts.

15.notes: Firethorn. Gene Sarazen's 'Shot Heard Round the World' - double eagle (albatross) in 1935. Pond fronts entire green. Risk-reward second shot.

16.notes: Redbud. Water guards left and front. Sunday pin on far left. Tiger Woods' chip-in 2005. Jack Nicklaus' famous 1-iron.

17.notes: Nandina. Former Eisenhower Tree location (removed 2014). Uphill approach to severely two-tiered green. Miss the tier, face 60-foot putt.

18.notes: Holly. Iconic uphill finisher with clubhouse backdrop. Two deep greenside bunkers demand accuracy. Countless Green Jacket dramas have unfolded here.

1.notes: Straightforward opener. Water left catches pulled drives.

2.notes: Reachable par 5. Water left threatens aggressive line to green.

3.notes: Short par 3 with water surrounding green. Club selection critical.

4.notes: Shorter par 4. Position A is right-center for best angle.

5.notes: Long par 4. Avoid the fairway bunkers to have reasonable approach.

6.notes: Mid-length par 4. Bunker complex right of green demands accuracy.

7.notes: Tough par 4. SI #1. Fairway bunkers squeeze landing area.

8.notes: Long par 3. Water behind green penalizes long shots.

9.notes: Reachable par 5. Water guards left side through the green.

10.notes: Solid par 4 to start back nine. Favor right side off tee.

11.notes: Par 5 with water down left side. Play safe right.

12.notes: Shorter par 4. Birdie opportunity before the demanding finish.

13.notes: Short par 3. Water behind green. Don't be long.

14.notes: Demanding par 4. Long approach to narrow green.

15.notes: Tough par 4. Bunker right catches bailout off tee.

16.notes: Par 5 with water left. Lay up possible before demanding finish.

17.notes: THE ISLAND GREEN. Most famous par 3 in golf. 137 yards all carry over water. Tiny green.

18.notes: Strong finishing hole. Water left entire hole. Dramatic finish.

1.notes: Downhill opener along Monterey coastline. Bunker left off tee.

2.notes: Par 5 (par 4 in U.S. Open). OB right along beach.

3.notes: Uphill par 4 along coast. OB left.

4.notes: Short par 4. Beach/OB right of green. Stunning ocean views.

5.notes: Par 3 with views of Carmel Bay. Undulating green.

6.notes: Clifftop par 5. Spectacular ocean views. Dramatic approach over cliff.

7.notes: ICONIC par 3. Only 109 yards but tiny green perched on cliff. One of most photographed holes in golf.

8.notes: Dramatic approach shot across ocean inlet. Blind tee shot uphill.

9.notes: Par 4 along cliff edge. OB left entire hole.

10.notes: Dramatic oceanside par 4. SI #3. Cliff left entire hole.

11.notes: Inland par 4. Respite from ocean holes before demanding finish.

12.notes: Par 3 back inland. Bunkers guard both sides of green.

13.notes: Par 4 with wide fairway. Bunker left catches aggressive line.

14.notes: SI #1. Long par 5 dogleg right. OB right entire hole.

15.notes: Mid-length par 4. Bunkers both sides of green.

16.notes: Par 4 leading to iconic finish. Long narrow green.

17.notes: Iconic hourglass-shaped green. Nicklaus hit flagstick with 1-iron in '72. Watson's chip-in '82.

18.notes: Epic finishing hole. Ocean left entire hole. One of the most famous closing holes in golf.

1.notes: Straightaway opening par 4 toward the Pacific Ocean. Fairway bunkers on both sides at driving distance. Tree-lined with canyon on left.

2.notes: Medium-length par 4 with bunkers guarding the approach. Views toward ocean on right.

3.notes: Iconic par 3 along the Pacific cliffs. Ocean views and dramatic bunkering. One of the most photographed holes in golf.

4.notes: Long par 4 along the ocean cliffs. One of the most scenic and demanding holes. Stay left off the tee to avoid cliff danger.

5.notes: Uphill par 4 heading back inland. Solid approach shot needed to hold elevated green.

6.notes: Reachable par 5 with dogleg left. Risk-reward options for long hitters. Deep bunker guards left side of green.

7.notes: Dogleg right par 4. Tiger Woods made legendary putt here in 2008 U.S. Open playoff. Uphill second shot to well-bunkered green.

8.notes: Short par 3 with bunkers surrounding the green. Plays slightly downhill.

9.notes: Longest par 5 on the course at 615 yards. Uphill the entire way. Strategic bunker placement demands precision.

10.notes: Back nine opens with a solid par 4. Downhill tee shot allows for aggressive line.

11.notes: Long par 3 with bunkers surrounding the green. Club selection critical with downhill lie.

12.notes: Dogleg left par 5. Uphill second and third shots. Well-protected green demands accurate approach.

13.notes: Downhill par 5 with dogleg right. Good opportunity for birdie but bunkers punish wayward shots.

14.notes: Uphill par 4. Requires solid long iron or hybrid approach into elevated green.

15.notes: Long par 4 with narrow deep green. Precision approach required for birdie opportunity.

16.notes: Long par 3 playing uphill. Bunkers guard entire perimeter of green. Club up for elevation.

17.notes: Solid par 4 setting up dramatic finish. Fairway bunker left demands accurate tee shot.

18.notes: Dramatic finishing par 5 with water guarding the green. Jon Rahm won 2021 U.S. Open with birdie here. Risk-reward approach shot.

1.notes: Opening par 4 at Arnold Palmer's Bay Hill. Tree-lined fairway with bunkers at driving distance.

2.notes: Long par 3 with water behind the green. Classic Dick Wilson design with bunkers protecting the front.

3.notes: Solid par 4. Avoid fairway bunker right for clean approach to elevated green.

4.notes: Dogleg left par 5. Water guards the left side making it risk-reward for those trying to reach in two.

5.notes: Medium-length par 4. Accuracy off the tee important to avoid fairway bunker left.

6.notes: Dogleg right par 5 along the lake. Water right threatens aggressive line. Classic Bay Hill risk-reward.

7.notes: Short par 3 with water behind. Club selection critical - don't go long.

8.notes: Long par 4. Fairway bunker right influences tee shot strategy.

9.notes: Strong par 4 finishing the front nine. Sets up the challenging back nine.

10.notes: Back nine opens with straightaway par 4. Solid start before water-laden stretch.

11.notes: Medium-length par 4 with bunkers influencing play.

12.notes: Par 5 along the lake. Water left entire way makes this one of Bay Hill's signature holes.

13.notes: Short par 4 offering birdie chance before the tough finish.

14.notes: Par 3 with water fronting the green. One of the most photographed holes at Bay Hill.

15.notes: Par 4 leading into the famous finishing stretch.

16.notes: Signature par 5. Water right threatens entire hole. Scene of many Arnold Palmer Invitational heroics.

17.notes: Par 3 over water. One of the most intimidating tee shots in golf. Precision required.

18.notes: Iconic finishing hole. Water guards the entire right side and fronts the green. One of golf's most dramatic finishes.

1.notes: Opening par 4 at TPC Scottsdale. Desert layout with bunkers guarding driving zone. Set the tone early.

2.notes: Long par 4. Fairway bunker right guards driving zone. Solid approach needed.

3.notes: Reachable par 5 with dogleg left. Desert waste area guards left side. Risk-reward decision off the tee.

4.notes: Short par 3. Bunkers and waste area surround the green. Precision required.

5.notes: Long par 4. Fairway bunkers on both sides make accuracy essential.

6.notes: Par 4 with bunker right off the tee. Avoid fairway bunker for clean approach.

7.notes: Par 3 over water. Club selection critical. Bunkers behind and left punish miss-hits.

8.notes: Long par 4. Bunkers squeeze the landing area. Accurate driving essential.

9.notes: Strong finishing par 4 for front nine. Sets up the back nine challenge.

10.notes: Back nine opens with solid par 4. Fairway bunker left influences tee shot.

11.notes: Long par 4. Number 1 handicap hole on the course. Demands accuracy and length.

12.notes: Par 3 with water fronting the green. Bunkers guard the back. Club selection critical.

13.notes: Dogleg right par 5. Water guards right side entire way. Risk-reward for those going for green in two.

14.notes: Long par 4. Bunkers on both sides challenge the drive. Solid par is a good score.

15.notes: Reachable par 5 with dogleg left. Water left demands respect. Great birdie opportunity or big number.

16.notes: THE STADIUM HOLE. Most famous par 3 in tournament golf. 20,000-capacity grandstands fully surround the hole creating a coliseum atmosphere. No water - just desert bunkers. Tiger Woods hole-in-one in 1997. Crowd goes wild for makes, boos for misses.

17.notes: Short par 4. Driveable for long hitters but bunkers guard approach. Birdie opportunity before tough 18th.

18.notes: Dramatic finishing hole with water left off the tee and guarding the green. Strong par 4 demands accuracy to close out the round.

1.notes: Tea Olive-style opener. Tee elevated 75ft above fairway. Par 5. OB left, barranca crosses fairway. Tempting to hit driver but requires caution. Scores range eagle to double bogey.

2.notes: #1 handicap hole. Long par 4 playing uphill into wind. Most difficult hole on course. Hogan's Alley signature challenge.

3.notes: Medium-length par 4 playing into breeze. Accuracy off tee essential.

4.notes: World famous par 3. Ben Hogan called it 'the best par three in America'. Long carry, bunkers surround green.

5.notes: Plays into wind. Almost on side of hill. Challenging approach angle.

6.notes: SIGNATURE HOLE. World-famous for bunker IN MIDDLE of green. Decision point: putt around or chip over bunker if on wrong side of green.

green.notes: Bunker in center of green - unique in golf

7.notes: Narrow fairway difficult to hit. Tight tree-lined corridor demands accuracy.

8.notes: Two fairways separated by dry ditch (barranca). Must choose bailout or aggressive line. Long par 4.

9.notes: Long uphill finish to front nine. Well-placed fairway bunkers demand strategic tee shot.

10.notes: Very short 315-yard par 4. Risk-reward. Long hitters can drive green but bunkers surround it. Known for drama.

11.notes: Long par 5. Eucalyptus trees and barranca in play. Challenging second shot placement.

12.notes: Long par 4, often into wind. Narrow green surrounded by barranca, bunkers, and trees. Bogey's Tree (named after Humphrey Bogart).

13.notes: Tough driving hole. Barranca on left, eucalyptus on right. Precision required off tee.

14.notes: Easy par 3 with Riviera's largest green. Multi-tiered - putting difficult. Must be on correct tier.

green.notes: Riviera's largest and most tiered green

15.notes: Dogleg right par 4. Large two-tiered green. Plays into Pacific Ocean breeze. Often pivotal in determining champion.

green.notes: Two-tiered - must be on proper level

16.notes: Last par 3. Small green surrounded by bunkers. Can yield birdies but precision essential.

17.notes: Riviera's longest hole. Uphill, downwind, northeast towards clubhouse. Strategic second shot crucial.

18.notes: SIGNATURE FINISH. World-famous uphill finishing hole. Blind tee shot. Finding fairway crucial to reach green in regulation. Natural amphitheater with clubhouse view.

1.notes: Opening par 4. Wide landing area but strategic approach to elevated green.

2.notes: Short par 3. Strategic bunker placement guards the green.

3.notes: Demanding par 4 with tight landing area and strategic approach.

4.notes: Long par 4 demanding accuracy off the tee.

5.notes: #4 handicap hole. Challenging par 4 with water hazard potential on miss.

6.notes: Reachable par 5 for long hitters. Risk-reward with water in play.

7.notes: #2 handicap hole. Long, demanding par 4. Donald Ross design challenge.

8.notes: Long par 4 with strategic bunker placement.

9.notes: Long par 3 finishing front nine. Bunkers guard green.

10.notes: Opening back nine par 4. Wide landing area.

11.notes: Short par 3 over water. Precision required.

12.notes: Medium par 4 with strategic approach.

13.notes: #3 handicap hole. Demanding par 4 with water threat.

14.notes: Par 4 with water on right side. Strategic tee shot essential.

15.notes: Short par 3. Water guards front. Small target.

16.notes: #1 handicap hole. Long, demanding par 4.

17.notes: Medium par 4. Sets up dramatic finish.

18.notes: SIGNATURE FINISH. Long par 5 uphill to clubhouse. Bobby Jones' home course. FedEx Cup drama finale. Water guards approach.

hazards.notes: Lake guards approach - iconic FedEx Cup finish

1.notes: Opening par 4. Straight drive imperative through 30-yard chute of overhanging branches. Fairway opens in landing area. Short/medium iron to green slanting front to back. Front left bunker guards green.

2.notes: Reachable par 5 for long hitters who keep drive left. Otherwise position second shot down left to open diagonal green. Bunkers left and long of green.

3.notes: Trees line both sides. Straight shot essential. Large bunker guards front of green with four more right. Small green hard to hold. Par is great score.

4.notes: Classic Pete Dye risk/reward par 3. Water guards left side. Ample bailout right. Hidden bunker behind green can save from lagoon.

5.notes: Aim right of left fairway bunker. Oak tree guards front of green. Massive strategic bunker right. Water and bunkers left. Deep narrow green.

6.notes: Carry fairway bunker right for best angle. Green tilts back to front. Bunkers both sides.

7.notes: Long par 3 surrounded by sand. Trees the real hazard - reject off-line shots. Narrow but deep green. Pin placement affects club selection.

8.notes: #1 handicap hole. Toughest on course. Well-placed drive essential to avoid water and strategic trees. Carry dogleg past first two pines for shot at elusive green.

9.notes: Short but deceptive par 4 (325 yards). Placement over power. Drive to middle to avoid tree battles on approach. Heart-shaped green protected front and behind by sand.

10.notes: Wide landing area invites big drive but temper gusto - lagoon lurks left. Medium/long iron to green with two strategic bunkers.

11.notes: Trees and water lurk both sides of fairway chute. Long/medium iron to green, avoiding trees protecting right side. Bunkers flank green.

12.notes: Reaching the dogleg off tee imperative for par chance. Medium/long iron to green. Back pin placement can be 3-club difference. Two bunkers final challenge.

13.notes: Tee shot positioned right of fairway to set up approach between two large oaks. Large cypress planked bunker guards front of green.

14.notes: Beautiful aquatic par 3. Water from tee to green on right. Green is small, isolated target with two small pot bunkers as magnets. Overhanging trees complicate.

15.notes: Thinking challenge par 5. Demands careful deliberation and shot execution. Water left, zealous bunker-guardians both sides.

16.notes: Exclamation point of sand says it all on this dogleg left. Avoid left, beware slalom pines right. Short iron to green. Cluster of bunkers right can humble par-seekers.

17.notes: SIGNATURE HOLE. Water nearly everywhere. Bold iron shot must be accurate. 90-yard bunker can save slightly off-center shot from watery extension. Head/crosswinds normal.

18.notes: SIGNATURE FINISH. One of golf's most recognizable finishes with Harbour Town Lighthouse behind green. Wide landing area juts into Calibogue Sound. Long approach with ample bailout right. Insidious mounding right causes many lost pars.

1.notes: Opening par 4 at Muirfield Village. Slight downhill tee shot to generous fairway. Nicklaus design rewards smart positioning.

2.notes: Challenging uphill par 4. Creek crosses in front of green. Precision required on approach.

3.notes: Dogleg left par 4. Water guards left side. Play center-right off tee.

4.notes: Stunning downhill par 3 over water. One of Nicklaus's signature holes. Club selection critical.

5.notes: Reachable par 5 with risk-reward second shot. Creek guards left side of green.

6.notes: Strong par 4 with water left. Play safe to center-right of fairway.

7.notes: Long par 5, slight dogleg right. Water in play on layup and approach. Strategic three-shot hole.

8.notes: Short par 3 to elevated green. Bunkers surround putting surface. Back pin is treacherous.

9.notes: Closing hole of front nine. Water guards left. Strong finish required.

10.notes: Demanding start to back nine. Water left, bunkers right. Precision essential.

11.notes: Long par 5 with dramatic elevation change. Creek crosses twice. Birdie opportunity for long hitters.

12.notes: Iconic par 3 over water. Similar to Augusta's 12th. Wind swirls make club selection difficult.

13.notes: Demanding par 4 with water right. Favor left side off tee. Approach to elevated green.

14.notes: Short par 4 with big-hitting opportunity. Water guards right side of green. Precision rewarded.

15.notes: Reachable par 5 with significant risk. Water guards entire left side. Bold play can yield eagle.

16.notes: Dramatic par 3 over water to peninsula green. One of golf's most photographed holes.

17.notes: Demanding par 4 with water right. Green slopes severely. Birdie or bogey hole.

18.notes: Dramatic finishing hole. Creek crosses fairway and guards green. Theater for champions.

14.notes: Long par 4 dogleg left. One of Colonial's toughest holes. Requires two quality shots.

16.notes: Short par 3 over water. Wind can affect club selection significantly.

17.notes: Par 4 dogleg right with water threat. Position off tee is critical.

1.notes: Par 4 opener. One of narrowest fairways on course but ample room between sand area on right and dune grass on left. Good drive leaves short iron to gently undulating green tucked into natural dune area. Pete Dye: 'Furthest from ocean, hit driver freely.'

2.notes: Par 5 with two marsh crossings. Decide how much to bite off on tee shot. Elevated green set between two sand ridges, reachable in two depending on wind. Pete Dye: 'Nothing but sand around the green.'

3.notes: Par 4. Fire across marsh from island tee to extremely wide fairway. Best drives find plateau on left for best look at elevated tabletop green. Half wedge can be treacherous. Miss short or right for difficult up-and-down.

4.notes: Most difficult hole on outward nine. Par 4. Second shot to large green can require nearly every club depending on wind. Bailout area front left. #1 handicap hole. Pete Dye: 'Strictly a driving hole.'

5.notes: Par 3. Hourglass green running away diagonally from right. Large natural sand area from tee to green with steep face cutting into middle. Largest green on course. Must hit appropriate portion or face difficult two-putt.

hazards.notes: Runs from tee to green, steep face cuts into middle

6.notes: Par 4. Perfect drive at center oak with slight draw, avoiding sand area and pond to left. Green open in front, protected left and right by sand. Narrow, deep green with undulation.

7.notes: Par 5. Wind determines strategy for tee shot - decide whether to carry natural dune area intruding from right or play left. Slightly elevated green open in front. Good birdie chance downwind.

hazards.notes: Runs down left side and around back of green

8.notes: Par 3. Seemingly simple but becomes more difficult as pin is cut further into elevated green. Framed by live oaks front left. Narrow as it extends from tee. Miss long or right finds sand. Back third of green 3ft lower.

hazards.notes: Behind green, shouldn't come into play

9.notes: Par 4. Wide fairway sloping down from right makes driving length more important than direction. Green offset - ball must be turning right to left or carry to green. Grassy swale and deep sand areas left and right.

10.notes: Par 4. Elevated tee atop sand dune with panorama of back nine. Drive left-center to crest sets up second shot to green set down into dunes. Large sand area front left, steep faced sand area to back.

11.notes: Par 5. Key is accurate lay-up. Avoid sand areas right of fairway off tee. Second shot: lay back on right side, taking sand area left out of play. Elevated green atop dune ridge.

12.notes: Par 4. Widest fairway on course but gives way to one of narrowest approaches. Good drive sets up downhill second. Approach appears narrow but open in front with rolling collar area left.

13.notes: Par 4. May be most difficult hole on inward nine. Canal runs down entire right side - comes into play on tee shots. Two fairway pot bunkers in play. Green long and narrow with water right, bunkers left.

14.notes: Par 3. Perhaps most beautiful hole but also one of most treacherous. Severely exposed and elevated green. Most exposed putting surface on entire course. Miss front right only safe spot. Dynamic ocean views from tee.

15.notes: Par 4. Seemingly straightforward but often overlooked. Tee shot must find fairway for mid-iron to green set in natural dune area. Difficult to determine wind speed and direction in sheltered green.

16.notes: Par 5. Downwind provides great chance for birdie or eagle - many go for green in two. Extremely deep and dangerous sand area guards left side of green. Advantage to play down right side.

17.notes: Par 3. Pete Dye's favorite hole. All carry over water. Target appears narrow, fiercely guarded by water short and right, two deep sand areas left. Almost a double green with front and back landing areas.

18.notes: Par 4. Best drives hug right side of fairway. Wind determines whether players fire with mid irons or long irons/fairway metals. Dunes on right more severe than any other hole. Pete Dye considers it best par 4 on course.

14.notes: Par 4. 451 yards from blacks. #6 handicap. Long par 4 with bunkers guarding both sides of fairway and green.

17.notes: Par 5. 520 yards from blacks. #10 handicap. Sweeping dogleg left with excellent birdie opportunity for aggressive play. Ocean views.

2.notes: 469-yard par 4 with slight right movement. Desert wash runs along the left. One of the harder holes on the front.

3.notes: 492-yard par 5. Reachable for long hitters but water short of green demands precision. Featured hole on the Shriners Open.

4.notes: 450-yard par 4 with slight left movement. Stroke index 3 - one of the more demanding holes on the course.

5.notes: 197-yard downhill par 3. Green surrounded by bunkers. Desert landscape with mountain views.

6.notes: 430-yard par 4 dogleg right. Stroke index 1 - most difficult hole on the course. Desert wash guards the right side.

8.notes: 239-yard downhill par 3. Long and challenging with bunkers surrounding the green. Tiered putting surface.

9.notes: 563-yard par 5 dogleg left. Reachable in two but water guards the front of the green. Major scoring swing potential.

10.notes: 420-yard par 4 to start the back nine. Desert waste area on left demands accuracy off the tee.

11.notes: 448-yard par 4 with slight left movement. Stroke index 2 - demanding second shot to well-protected green.

12.notes: 442-yard par 4. Desert waste areas on both sides create a demanding driving corridor.

4.notes: Shortest hole on course. Small undulating green guarded by large bunkers front, left, and right. Finding correct level crucial.

5.notes: Par 5 reachable in two with wind. Monstrous bunker right, three bunkers left off tee. Small pot bunker front of green, large bunker left.

6.notes: Longest par 4. Slight dogleg right with deep bunkers right and large bunkers front of green. Run-up option available. 2019 Senior Open 18th hole.

7.notes: Risk/reward par 4. Set of bunkers forces decision - lay up short or take them on. Large green with severe back to front slope.

8.notes: Tree-lined par 4 requiring carry over ravine and bunkers. False front on rectangular green. Plays into wind. Generous landing area.

9.notes: Deceptive par 3. Large bunker appears greenside but is 20 yards short. Take half club more. Aim at clubhouse chimney.

10.notes: Par 5 with options. Drive carrying crest allows reaching in two. Creek front and left of green. Massive false front. Bail out right of green.

11.notes: Longest par 3, largest green on course. Bunkers line fairway and border green. False front demands carrying shot onto surface.

12.notes: Long difficult par 4. Trees both sides, bunkers in landing area. Par is very good score. Requires big tee shot.

13.notes: Long dogleg left. Bunkers guard left side. Right side of fairway gives best approach angle. Green accepts running approach.

14.notes: Last par 3. Well-bunkered green with large bunker left and hidden bunker right. False front demands carrying shot. Condoleezza Rice hole-in-one here.

15.notes: Three bunkers right, two large bunkers left frame landing zone. T-shaped green with large greenside bunkers. Carry to wide part of fairway.

16.notes: Signature hole. Tee shot over Juday Creek, heavy bunkering left. Short uphill approach to severely undulating green with front pot bunker. 2019 Senior Open opening hole.

17.notes: Last par 5. Elevated tee to fairway bordered by pond/wetlands on left. Uphill second shot to one of more narrow, small greens. Miss short for bogey.

18.notes: Strong finishing hole. Dogleg left requires carry over Juday Creek. Waste area runs entire left side. Large cottonwood tree blocks right approach. Bill Coore/Ben Crenshaw restored after 2016 flood.

1.notes: Reachable par 5 with dramatic 40-foot elevated tee shot over a road. Very deep green. Keep tee shot right to avoid left bunker. Birdie opportunity.

2.notes: Tough driving hole. 230 yards to carry left bunker. Both fairway bunkers are very difficult - avoid at all cost. Bogey is acceptable.

3.notes: Long challenging par 3. Hazard left, tall trees right. Green has ridge running down center. Downhill putts roll out. Birdie is rare.

4.notes: Short but often tough par 3. Pin up front leaves little room between tight left bunker and hazard right. Avoid double bogey.

5.notes: Solid dogleg right par 4. Play left to avoid getting blocked by trees. Large elevated green slopes left. Birdie hole with well-placed drive.

6.notes: #1 handicap hole. Good drive required. Creek 70-80 yards short of green catches many shots. Back of green slopes away. Par is excellent.

7.notes: Good birdie hole. 240+ yard drive to clear corner. Green reachable in 2 with good position. Very deep green slopes back to front.

8.notes: Short par 3 with big green across hazard. Check wind carefully - tee is sheltered. Easiest of five par 3s. Good birdie chance.

9.notes: Great uphill dogleg. Aim at right corner of bunker (250 yds). All tee shots kick right. Hazard down entire right side. Back-to-front sloping green.

10.notes: Strong uphill par 4 plays longer than yardage. Favor left side on drive. 220+ to clear right bunker. Add a club on approach. Deep two-tiered green.

11.notes: Short par 4 with choices off tee. Right bunker ~240 yds, left bunker shorter. Big hitters can nearly drive green. Back of green slopes away.

12.notes: Signature hole with two large oaks in middle of wide fairway. Top players drive left side for open 2nd shot. Deep green slopes away at back. Birdies rare.

13.notes: Straight-away par 4. Wide fairway for long hitters who can carry left bunker (~240 yds). OB right in play. Avoid short bunker 50 yds from green.

14.notes: Mid-length par 3 more challenging than it appears. Check wind from previous hole - protected at tee. Stay below the hole. Avoid short-siding.

15.notes: Short dogleg-right. Middle bunker ~250 yds, right bunker ~280 yds. Longer drives (270+) leave 40-80 yd pitch. Stay below the pin.

16.notes: Straightaway uphill par 5, only reachable by long hitters. Fairway bunker requires 280 yd drive to clear. Safer down left side. Very deep green.

17.notes: Uphill par 3 to large two-tier green. Often helping wind but plays longer. Avoid front bunker - par is rare from there. Fast back-to-front on lower tier.

18.notes: Downhill finish with SF views on clear days. Wide fairway but trouble left from trees. Sloping lies make 2nd shot tough. Clear the right bunker.

1.notes: Opening par 4 with panoramic Virginia countryside views. Similar to original Birdwood layout. 372 yards from Cavalier tees.

2.notes: Only par-5 on front nine. Morey Creek guards green. Risk-reward for going in two - bunker and creek bank collect wayward shots. 574 yards.

3.notes: Scot Sherman's tribute to Pete Dye. Similar to 18th at Pete Dye Golf Club. Partially blind tee shot with pond. One of the most difficult holes. 439 yards.

4.notes: Seth Raynor/C.B. Macdonald Leven template (like 17th at National Golf Links). Fairway bunkers and pine trees on preferred line. Water behind green. 485 yards par 4.

5.notes: Reverse Cape template (like 5th at Mid Ocean in Bermuda). Ultimate risk-reward tee shot. Babe Ruth once hit 20 balls in the lake trying to drive the green. 423 yards.

6.notes: Short par 4 with wetlands flanking entire left side. Large rock shelf discovered during construction shifted green forward. 358 yards.

7.notes: Scot Sherman's 'field hole' - strategy developed in the field. Views back toward Ragged Mountain are memorable. 159 yards par 3.

8.notes: Most talked about hole at Birdwood - love it or hate it. Strategic layup crucial to clear ravine. Smallest green on course, essentially an island surrounded by native rocks. 370 yards.

hazards.notes: Native rocks and natural areas surround green

9.notes: Biarritz template (like 9th at Yale). Requires accurate long tee shot. Green is very long with swale in middle - can cause 4-5 club difference per pin. 246 yards par 3.

10.notes: Longest hole on course. Davis Love III's favorite - he personally directed fairway shaping. Deep swale bisects front half of green. 615 yards par 5.

11.notes: Most beautiful hole on course. Reminiscent of Pete Dye's par-3s at Whistling Straits. Looks like nowhere to land but the green. Bunkers help offline shots. 248 yards par 3.

12.notes: Most popular hole at Birdwood. Tee shot from ridge with widest fairway on course. Going for green in two risks tricky chip from collection area left. 549 yards par 5.

13.notes: 'Buried elephant' in middle of green (originally planned as bunker like Riviera's 6th). Green contouring is the trick - two-putts are a challenge. 209 yards par 3.

14.notes: One of the most challenging holes. Native area right is now more in play on approach. Iconic water tower at Birdwood Mansion visible from green. 461 yards.

15.notes: Last challenging par 5. Requires precise tee shot - fairway seems to get narrower all the way to green. Creek on right catches wayward approaches. Smallest bunker on course guards front right pin. 572 yards.

16.notes: Davis Love III wanted legitimate drivable par-4 late in round for last birdie chance. Old catalpa tree impacts shots from 125+ yards. Pot bunker in middle of fairway. Most photographed spot - pond and Birdwood Mansion view. 349 yards.

17.notes: Final diverse par 3. Guarded by beautiful native rock wall. Water tower and Birdwood Mansion (built 1819-1830) make memorable setting. 240 yards.

18.notes: Long, uphill closing hole into prevailing wind. One of few truly uphill approaches adds drama. Par is always a good finishing score. 447 yards.

1.notes: Demanding opener. Pete Dye signature with railroad ties fronting fairway bunker left. River visible right. Favor right side off tee.

2.notes: Challenging uphill par 3 to a severely sloped green. Back tier plays two clubs longer. Avoid right bunker - difficult recovery.

3.notes: Risk-reward par 5. Cut corner over waste area for eagle chance, or lay up left. Green guarded by Dye's trademark bunker complex.

4.notes: Strong par 4 with river along entire left side. Bailout right but brings bunkers into play. Green slopes toward water.

5.notes: Uphill par 4 requiring precise tee shot. Bunkers guard landing area both sides. Green perched on hillside with dramatic views.

6.notes: Short par 4 with island-style green. Water surrounds three sides. Layup to 100 yards or attack with mid-iron. Precision paramount.

hazards.notes: Pond surrounds green front and sides

7.notes: Reachable par 5 for long hitters who can carry the corner. Creek crosses 80 yards short. Three-putt risk with back pin.

8.notes: Beautiful par 3 over wetland. No bailout - all carry to green. Wind significantly impacts club selection. Avoid back bunker.

9.notes: Strong finishing hole for front nine. Drive must avoid fairway bunkers to set up approach. Elevated green with false front.

10.notes: Birdie opportunity to start back nine. Wide fairway but deep bunkers await offline shots. Green receptive with back-to-front slope.

11.notes: Classic Pete Dye risk-reward. Aggressive line over bunkers shortens hole but OB lurks left. Play safe right for bogey-free par.

12.notes: Long demanding par 4. Plays even longer uphill. Large green but well-protected. #1 handicap hole. Par is excellent score.

13.notes: Short par 4 with premium on accuracy. Creek guards right side of green. Left bunker is deep with sod wall face.

14.notes: Long par 3 playing over ravine. Wind swirls in valley. Green has severe right-to-left slope - aim right and let ball feed.

15.notes: Birdie chance with good drive. Waste area right tempts aggressive line. Green slopes away from approach - flight ball high.

16.notes: Signature hole. Dramatic downhill par 3 with river behind green. Club selection critical - wind off water affects ball flight.

17.notes: Strategic par 5 requiring three well-placed shots. Bunker complex at 300 yards dictates layup strategy. Elevated green rewards precision.

18.notes: Dramatic finisher. River runs entire left side. Fairway slopes toward water - favor right. Green complex with Dye's railroad tie wall.

1.notes: Opening hole drops 40 feet from tee to green. Maxwell's trademark undulating fairway. Creek crosses 60 yards short of green.

2.notes: Stout uphill par 4. Plays 20 yards longer than yardage suggests. Green features pronounced Maxwell roll - putt breaks more than expected.

3.notes: Downhill par 3 with stunning views. Wind swirls in valley. Green has severe undulation - two distinct tiers. Match tier or three-putt risk.

4.notes: Classic Maxwell dogleg. Expansive fairway rewards smart positioning. Green sits in natural amphitheater with dramatic Maxwell contours.

5.notes: Birdie hole if you find fairway. Wide landing area but creek right threatens approach. False front rejects weak shots.

6.notes: Challenging uphill par 5 with blind second shot. Creek meanders through landing area. Green slopes severely back to front.

7.notes: Medium par 3 to one of Maxwell's finest greens. Famous 'Maxwell roll' runs through center - putts break dramatically. Club to flag location.

8.notes: Sweeping downhill par 4 with dramatic views. Fairway humps and hollows create awkward lies. Green perched on shelf.

9.notes: Strong finishing hole for front nine. Creek left of green creates risk-reward on approach. Par is good score heading to clubhouse.

10.notes: Wide driving hole to start back nine. Green sits below fairway with dramatic Maxwell contours. Back pin extremely difficult.

11.notes: Roller coaster par 5 with fairway that rises and falls. Second shot blind over ridge. Green tucked in valley with creek short.

12.notes: Demanding uphill par 4. #1 handicap. Wide fairway narrows near green. Approach must carry bunkers to access pins.

13.notes: Short par 4 with big green. Tee shot must avoid creek left. Green has pronounced ridge - wrong side means long putt.

14.notes: Long par 3 to elevated green. Club selection tricky with swirling winds. Green surrounded by bunkers - find putting surface or struggle.

15.notes: Short par 4 tempting driver. Bunker guards corner. Green slopes severely toward creek - below hole position is key.

16.notes: Beautiful par 3 with green nestled in hollow. Ball feeds toward center from all sides. Maxwell's genius on display.

17.notes: Strong par 4 with demanding approach. Fairway tilts right toward bunkers. Green has three distinct areas - must hit correct one.

18.notes: Dramatic finisher returning to clubhouse. Sweeping vista from tee. Creek crosses 50 yards short - creates tension on approach. Maxwell's finest closing hole.

9.notes: Strategic par 4 closing front nine. Bunker guards inside of dogleg. Green features ridge - match pin location.

11.notes: Demanding uphill par 4. #1 handicap hole. Fairway narrows approaching green. Bogey is acceptable score.

12.notes: Reachable par 5 for long hitters. Bunker complex at corner dictates strategy. Green slopes right to left toward trees.

15.notes: Solid par 4 requiring two good shots. Tree-lined throughout. Green slopes back to front with subtle break.

16.notes: SIGNATURE HOLE. Dramatic 206-yard par 3 from elevated tee to elevated green. Overlooks entire course. Bunkers guard both sides. Wind swirls - club selection critical.

17.notes: Challenging uphill par 4. Dogleg left requires positioning. Green sits atop hill with panoramic views. Approach plays long.

18.notes: Downhill finishing hole back to clubhouse. Wide fairway rewards good drive. Green protected by bunkers - finishing birdie in reach.
